md: ## Ladder Problems
Ladder problems are everywhere in trigonometry — they are the cleanest real-world example of a right triangle. The ladder is the **hypotenuse**, the wall is one leg, and the ground is the other leg.

blocks.title: The mental picture
blocks.body: Wall is **vertical**, ground is **horizontal**, ladder leans across them. The right angle is between the wall and the ground.

md: ### Standard labels
**Hypotenuse** — the **ladder** itself.
**Vertical leg** — the **height up the wall** that the ladder reaches.
**Horizontal leg** — the **distance along the ground** from the wall to the ladder's base.
**Angle of inclination** — the angle the ladder makes with the **ground**.
### Which ratio do you use?
It depends on which two of the three quantities (ladder length, wall height, ground distance) appear in the problem.
**ladder + wall height** → uses **opposite & hypotenuse** → **sine**.
**ladder + ground distance** → uses **adjacent & hypotenuse** → **cosine**.
**wall height + ground distance** → uses **opposite & adjacent** → **tangent**.
### Worked example
*A 10-foot ladder leans against a wall. The angle the ladder makes with the ground is $65°$. How high up the wall does it reach?*
**Picture it.** Ladder $= 10$ (hyp). Wall height $= h$ (opp). Angle $= 65°$.
**Pick the ratio.** opp + hyp + angle → sine.
$$\sin 65° = \dfrac{h}{10} \Rightarrow h = 10 \sin 65° \approx 10 \cdot 0.906 \approx 9.06 \text{ ft}$$

blocks.title: Safety check
blocks.body: Real-world ladders should make about a $75°$ angle with the ground. Trig problems don't always respect this — but if you get a wall height greater than the ladder length, you've made an error somewhere.

md: ## Guy Wire Problems

A **guy wire** is a tensioned cable that supports a tall, thin structure — a radio antenna, a utility pole, a tent, a mast on a sailboat. One end is attached to the structure (high up); the other end is anchored to the ground a distance away.
Geometrically, a guy wire creates a right triangle: the **wire is the hypotenuse**, the **structure is one leg**, the **ground is the other leg**.

blocks.title: Same shape as a ladder
blocks.body: A guy wire is just a ladder problem in disguise. The wire replaces the ladder; the pole replaces the wall. Same right triangle, same trig.

md: ### Standard labels
**Hypotenuse** — the **wire** (or cable) itself.
**Vertical leg** — the **height of the attachment point** on the structure.
**Horizontal leg** — the **distance from the base of the structure** to the anchor point on the ground.
**Angle** — usually the angle the wire makes with the ground (the **angle of elevation** along the wire).
### Worked example
*A guy wire is attached to the top of a $30$-foot pole and anchored to the ground $40$ feet from the base of the pole. How long is the wire?*
**Picture it.** Pole height $= 30$, ground distance $= 40$. We want the wire (hypotenuse).
**Pick the tool.** Two legs known → **Pythagorean theorem** (no trig needed).
(That's the famous $30$-$40$-$50$ — a $3$-$4$-$5$ scaled by 10.)

blocks.title: Multiple wires?
blocks.body: Real-world structures often have several wires anchored at different distances. Each wire forms its own right triangle — solve them independently.

md: ## Angles of Elevation & Depression
Many real-world right-triangle problems involve looking *up* at something tall or *down* at something below you. Two terms describe these viewing angles.

blocks.title: Two angles to know
blocks.body: **Angle of elevation:** the angle from the horizontal (your eye level) *up* to an object. **Angle of depression:** the angle from the horizontal *down* to an object.

md: ### A useful symmetry

If observer A looks *up* at observer B with elevation $\theta$, then B looks *down* at A with depression also $\theta$. They're alternate interior angles formed by the horizontal lines (which are parallel) and the line of sight (the transversal).
*Translation:* whenever you read 'angle of depression of $\theta$', you can equivalently use $\theta$ as the angle of elevation from the object below.
### The standard setup
**You** stand on the ground (or in a building, plane, lighthouse).
**Object** is at some height (or depth) some horizontal distance away.
Draw a horizontal line at your eye level. The line from you to the object makes the elevation/depression angle with that horizontal.
The right triangle has: horizontal distance (adjacent), height difference (opposite), and line of sight (hypotenuse).
### Worked example — elevation
*From a point on the ground, the angle of elevation to the top of a tree is $40°$. You are $25$ meters away from the tree. How tall is the tree?*
Adjacent (ground distance) $= 25$. Opposite (tree height) $= h$. Two legs → tangent.
$$\tan 40° = \dfrac{h}{25} \Rightarrow h = 25 \tan 40° \approx 25 \cdot 0.839 \approx 20.98 \text{ m}$$
### Worked example — depression
*A lighthouse keeper at a height of $60$ meters spots a boat with an angle of depression of $20°$. How far away (horizontally) is the boat?*
The angle of depression $20°$ equals the angle of elevation from the boat looking up.
Opposite (height) $= 60$. Adjacent (distance) $= d$. Two legs → tangent.
$$\tan 20° = \dfrac{60}{d} \Rightarrow d = \dfrac{60}{\tan 20°} \approx \dfrac{60}{0.364} \approx 164.85 \text{ m}$$

blocks.title: Drawing tip
blocks.body: Always sketch the picture before picking a ratio. Mark the horizontal as a dashed line, then the line of sight as a solid line, and label the angle between them. The triangle becomes obvious.

md: ## Bearings (Compass Directions)
A **bearing** is a direction expressed as an angle from a reference. They show up in navigation, surveying, hiking — anywhere you need to describe "which way" something is.
There are two common notations.
### Three-figure bearings (the standard)
A **three-figure bearing** is an angle measured **clockwise from North**, written as three digits ($000°$ to $360°$).
**Examples:** $000°$ → due North. $090°$ → due East. $180°$ → due South. $270°$ → due West. $045°$ → North-East. $135°$ → South-East.
Always pad to 3 digits ($045°$, not $45°$). This is what ships, planes, and the military use.

blocks.title: Always clockwise, always from North
blocks.body: Bearings rotate **clockwise** (the opposite of standard math angles, which are counterclockwise). And the reference is **North**, not East. So the same physical direction has different numbers in bearings vs standard-position angles.

md: ### Compass bearings (N-E-S-W form)

Older form, still common in textbooks. Written as **N $\theta°$ E**, **N $\theta°$ W**, **S $\theta°$ E**, or **S $\theta°$ W** — where $\theta$ is the *acute* angle between the direction and the nearest North/South line.
**Examples:** $\text{N } 30° \text{ E}$ means 30° east of due north. $\text{S } 60° \text{ W}$ means 60° west of due south.
Convert between the two notations:
$\text{N } 30° \text{ E}$ → $030°$ (30° clockwise from North)
$\text{S } 60° \text{ W}$ → $180° + 60° = 240°$ (south is 180°, then 60° more clockwise)
$\text{N } 45° \text{ W}$ → $360° - 45° = 315°$ (45° counterclockwise from North = 315° clockwise)
## Bearings & Direction Word Problems
Most intro bearings problems become **right-triangle problems** once you sketch the picture. The bearing tells you an angle; the distance tells you a side. SOHCAHTOA does the rest.
### Worked example 1 — straightforward right triangle
*A hiker walks $5$ km on a bearing of $060°$ from camp. How far **east** of camp is she?*
**Set up.** Bearing $060°$ means $60°$ clockwise from North. So her direction is $60°$ east of North — which means $90° - 60° = 30°$ from East.
Sketch a right triangle: hypotenuse = $5$ km (the path), one leg = east displacement ($x$), the other leg = north displacement.
The angle between her path and the North axis is $60°$, so the **east** displacement is the side **opposite** that $60°$ angle:
$$\sin 60° = \dfrac{x}{5} \Rightarrow x = 5\sin 60° \approx 5 \cdot 0.866 \approx 4.33 \text{ km east}$$
And her north displacement: $5\cos 60° = 2.5$ km north.
### Worked example 2 — multi-leg journey
*A boat sails $8$ km on a bearing of $090°$, then $6$ km on a bearing of $180°$. How far is it from the starting point, and on what bearing?*
**Picture.** Leg 1 is due East ($090°$) for $8$ km. Leg 2 is due South ($180°$) for $6$ km. The two legs are perpendicular — a clean right triangle!

**Bearing back to start (from the boat's current position):** the boat is $8$ km east and $6$ km south of start. Looking back from the boat: start is $8$ km west and $6$ km north — i.e., a bearing in the NW direction. The angle from North to the homeward direction is $\tan^{-1}(8/6) \approx 53.13°$ counterclockwise from North → bearing $\approx 360° - 53.13° = 306.87°$.

blocks.title: Strategy
blocks.body: Always **draw the picture** with North up. Convert bearings into angles within a right triangle. If the legs aren't perpendicular, you may need the law of sines/cosines (separate subtopic). For most intro problems, a bit of geometry exposes a right triangle.
